success_surface: Efficient bulk row filtering and deletion on large spreadsheets using vectorized pandas operations
effective_behaviors: Using vectorized boolean masking for multi-value filtering instead of row-by-row iteration
Selecting pandas over openpyxl for data transformation tasks where formulas and formatting preservation is not required
Implementing single-pass filtering logic with negated .isin() conditions for row exclusion
Including row count verification statistics before and after transformation for sanity checking
Addressing scalability concerns by explaining the computational efficiency of chosen approach
evidence: Used ~df['Group'].isin(target_groups) mask instead of iterating through rows individually
Applied pandas.read_excel() and to_excel() rather than openpyxl for faster processing on 13,000+ row file
Logged 'Original row count', 'Rows after filtering', and 'Rows deleted' for verification
Provided explicit explanation of why vectorized operations handle large files efficiently
Completed successfully with final row count: 206 -> 160 (46 rows deleted)

memory_items.title: Vectorized Row Filtering Pattern
memory_items.lesson: For bulk row operations on large datasets (>1000 rows), use boolean masks with .isin() or comparison operators instead of loops
memory_items.generalization: When removing/filtering multiple rows by condition, create inverted boolean masks with ~operator.in_condition() rather than iterative row deletion which triggers cascading recalculations

memory_items.title: Library Selection by Task Requirements
memory_items.lesson: Choose pandas for pure data manipulation tasks when formulas and cell styles do not need preservation
memory_items.generalization: Prefer pandas over openpyxl when speed is priority and format/formula retention is not required; pandas.to_excel() is significantly faster for bulk operations

memory_items.title: Verification Through Change Metrics
memory_items.lesson: Always log quantitative metrics showing input vs output state for transformations
memory_items.generalization: Report row counts, affected percentages, and delta values before saving transformed spreadsheets to enable quick validation

memory_items.title: Scalability-Aware Execution Strategy
memory_items.lesson: When users express performance concerns, prioritize solutions optimized for dataset size
memory_items.generalization: Select vectorized operations and memory-efficient methods when handling >10000 row spreadsheets; explain efficiency rationale to build user confidence

edits.content:
## Performance Optimization Patterns

### Bulk Row Operations
- **Vectorized Filtering**: For removing multiple rows (>100 total), use boolean masks with `~df[column].isin(values)` instead of iterative deletion. Single-pass operations prevent cascading recalculations.
- **Memory Efficiency**: Pandas C-backed operations outperform Python loops by 10-100x on datasets exceeding 1000 rows.

### Library Selection Heuristic
| Task Type | Recommended Tool | Rationale |
|-----------|------------------|----------|
| Data transformation + bulk row ops | pandas | Faster read/write, vectorized operations |
| Formula/format preservation | openpyxl | Retains cell-level metadata |
| Large files (>5000 rows) | pandas (priority) | Minimizes processing time |

### Verification Standards
- Log `len(df)` before and after any row-altering operation
- Calculate and report delta (rows added/removed)
- Include percentage change for context

### Scale-Aware Communication
- When users express performance concerns, explicitly address:
  - Time complexity of proposed solution
  - Why chosen approach scales better than alternatives
  - Expected completion time for their dataset size
